statement: In England, for people diagnosed with cancer via national screening programmes, 63.7% (around 11,600) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 2.1% (around 390) at stage IV.

In England, for people diagnosed with cancer by urgent GP two-week wait reeferral for suspected cancer symptoms, 33.1% (around 35,300) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 20.2% (around 21,500) at stage IV.

In England, for people diagnosed with cancer by routine or urgent GP referral (non-two-week wait), 40.8% (around 19,400) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 22.3% (around 10,600) at stage IV.

In England, for people diagnosed with cancer in an emergency, via emergency GP transfer to hospital as a hospital patient or via A&E, 13.9% (around  4,900) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 55.1% (around 19,500) at stage IV.
In England, for people diagnosed with cancer via other routes, 36.2% (around 10,100) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 27.1% (around  7,600) at stage IV.

In Northern Ireland, for people diagnosed with cancer in an emergency, via emergency GP transfer to hospital as a hospital patient or via A&E, 11.5% (around 190) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 55.6% (around 900) at stage IV.
source: Northern Ireland Cancer Registry, Queen's University Belfast

statement: In Northern Ireland, for people diagnosed with cancer by routine or urgent GP referral (non-two-week wait), 44.7% (around 760) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 18.9% (around 320) at stage IV.
source: Northern Ireland Cancer Registry, Queen's University Belfast

statement: In Northern Ireland, for people diagnosed with cancer via inpatient elective, 30.1% (around 55) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 35.5% (around 65) at stage IV.
source: Northern Ireland Cancer Registry, Queen's University Belfast

statement: In Northern Ireland, for people diagnosed with cancer via other outpatient, 43.2% (around 400) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 20.2% (around 190) at stage IV.
source: Northern Ireland Cancer Registry, Queen's University Belfast

statement: In Northern Ireland, for people diagnosed with cancer via national screening programmes, 61.7% (around 340) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 1.7% (around 10) at stage IV.
source: Northern Ireland Cancer Registry, Queen's University Belfast

statement: In Northern Ireland, for people diagnosed with cancer via suspected cancer (red flag), 33.6% (around 1,000) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 18.8% (around 560) at stage IV.
source: Northern Ireland Cancer Registry, Queen's University Belfast

statement: In Northern Ireland, for people diagnosed with cancer via unknown routes, 42.5% (around 90) of cancers were diagnosed at stage I compared to 26.6% (around 55) at stage IV.
source: Northern Ireland Cancer Registry, Queen's University Belfast
